content:
First, observe that any block of \(k\) consecutive 1’s contributes the integer
\[
c_k \;=\;\underbrace{11\ldots1}_{k\text{ times}}
\;=\;\frac{10^k-1}{9}.
\]
Since 
\[
c_1=1,\quad c_2=11,\quad c_3=111,\quad c_4=1111>1000,
\]
we can only use blocks of size \(1,2,3\).  Write
\[
x_1=a,\quad x_2=b,\quad x_3=c
\]
for the numbers of blocks of size \(1,2,3\).  Then the sum‐to‐1000 condition is
\[
1\cdot a+11\cdot b+111\cdot c=1000,
\]
and the total number of 1’s is
\[
n \;=\;a+2b+3c.
\]
From the first equation
\[
a=1000-11b-111c\;\ge0,
\]
so \((b,c)\) must satisfy \(11b+111c\le1000\).  Then
\[
n=(1000-11b-111c)+2b+3c
=1000-9b-108c
=1000-9\,(b+12c).
\]
Set \(d=b+12c\).  The nonnegativity \(a\ge0\) is equivalent to
\[
11b+111c=11(d-12c)+111c=11d-21c\le1000,
\]
and we also need \(b=d-12c\ge0\iff c\le d/12\).  One checks that the feasible
integer \(d\) range is exactly
\[
0\le d\le111,\quad 11d-21c\le1000,\quad c\le\lfloor d/12\rfloor
\]
has precisely \(108\) solutions in \(d\).  Hence there are \(108\) distinct
values of \(n\), namely
\[
n=1000-9d,\quad d\in D,\;\lvert D\rvert=108.
\]
Thus \(a=108\), and the sum of the digits of \(a\) is
\[
1+0+8=9.
\]
\boxed{9}
